claim: Single-user agents and multi-user agents require fundamentally different architectural approaches; transitioning from single-user to multi-user systems is not just adding users but engineering for isolation, cost controls, and observability.
actionable: When designing agents, clarify upfront whether you're building for one user or many, as this decision determines core architecture choices around state management, resource allocation, and monitoring.
source_title: Building Single-User vs Multi-User Agents: What Actually Changes

claim: Multi-user systems require hardened guardrails against prompt injection because multiple untrusted users can attack the system, whereas single-user systems (like personal OpenClaw instances) need minimal security hardening.
actionable: For multi-user agents, implement strict input validation and prompt injection defenses; for single-user agents, this overhead is unnecessary.
source_title: Building Single-User vs Multi-User Agents: What Actually Changes

claim: State collision and memory leakage between users is a critical failure mode in multi-user agents; shared caches or session keys must be strictly isolated per user to prevent privacy breaches.
actionable: Audit all caching and state storage mechanisms to ensure they use strong user-scoped keys; implement automated tests to detect cross-user data leakage.
source_title: Building Single-User vs Multi-User Agents: What Actually Changes

claim: Cost explosion is common in multi-user systems where one user's inefficient agent loops can burn through tokens/money; single-user systems tolerate this because the user adjusts their prompts, but multi-user systems need hard quota enforcements.
actionable: For production multi-user agents, implement per-user token budgets and per-request cost caps to prevent runaway spending.
source_title: Building Single-User vs Multi-User Agents: What Actually Changes
